# Everyday and preventive counselling {#sec-cases}

> *You have spent a year learning what to say. Now sit across from a patient and say it, in
> your own words, and see what comes back.*

These are **simulated-patient encounters**. In the choice-based cases of the earlier chapters
you drilled one idea at a time through set choices. Here you **talk with the patient in your own
words**, then get feedback on your counselling. They draw together the nutrition science,
communication, and clinical reasoning built up across the book.

This first set is the bread and butter of prevention: people who feel well, or nearly well, who
need a brief history, a myth gently corrected, and **one** realistic change they will actually make.

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
The patient is an **AI simulation for practice, not a real person, and not medical advice**.
Treat it as a natural consultation: ask, listen, and agree a realistic plan. If the simulated
patient is not enabled in your build, the encounter simply notes this and continues.
:::

## Mdm Tan — newly detected pre-diabetes

A 52-year-old teacher whose screening blood test came back with a raised fasting sugar.
Take a brief diet history, agree **one** realistic change with her, and check her confidence.

<div data-island="case" data-src="/cases/sim-prediabetes-mdm-tan.case.yml"></div>

## Mr Lim — "Doctor, I got a few questions"

A 41-year-old taxi driver whose screen flagged a borderline sugar and mildly high cholesterol.
He feels well but has a string of nutrition questions picked up from friends and online. Answer
them on quality-not-bans terms, and notice when a plan needs a dietitian.

<div data-island="case" data-src="/cases/sim-common-questions-mr-lim.case.yml"></div>

## Mr Tan — feels "basically fine"

A 38-year-old software engineer in for a pre-employment screen. He is asymptomatic and sceptical
anything is wrong. Engage him without lecturing, look beyond his diet, and see if you can agree
one realistic change.

<div data-island="case" data-src="/cases/sim-pillar-mr-tan.case.yml"></div>

## Mdm Goh — frightened of dementia

A 55-year-old teacher whose mother has dementia. She has a bag of brain supplements and a
borderline blood pressure and sugar she dismisses. Take her fear seriously, then lead with what
actually lowers the risk.

<div data-island="case" data-src="/cases/sim-dementia-prevention-mdm-goh.case.yml"></div>
